title: Analyzing a Simulated Labor Market
text: vignette walks complete analysis simulated labor market: simulate data, explore , estimate AKM model, decompose wage variance, correct limited mobility bias.

title: Analyzing a Simulated Labor Market
text: simulate labor market 5,000 workers observed 5 periods, moderate mobility (lambda = 0.15) Mincer wage equation:
code: library(labormarket) library(data.table)  set.seed(2024) lm_obj <- simlabormarket(   nk = 4,       # 4 firm types   nl = 5,       # 5 worker types   nt = 5,       # 5 time periods   ni = 5000,    # 5,000 workers   lambda = 0.15, # 15% moving probability   ratiog = 0.45, # 45% female   mincer = TRUE   # Mincer wage equation ) #> -- LaborMarket simulation --- #>  #>   Parameters #>     Firm types (nk):     4  #>     Worker types (nl):   5  #>     Time periods (nt):   5  #>     Individuals (ni):    5000  #>     Female ratio:        0.45  #>     Mobility (lambda):   0.15  #>  #>   Summary statistics #>     Observations:        25000  #>     Mean wage:           7.931  #>     Mean firm effect:    2.554  #>     Mean worker effect:  4.144  #>     Mean age:            38.7  #>     Mean education:      16.1  #>     Mean experience:     12.1  panel <- lm_obj@panel

title: Analyzing a Simulated Labor Market
code:
cat("Observations:", nrow(panel), "
") #> Observations: 25000 cat("Workers:", length(unique(panel$i)), "
") #> Workers: 5000 cat("Firms:", length(unique(panel$fid)), "
") #> Firms: 998 cat("Periods:", length(unique(panel$t)), "
") #> Periods: 5

title: Analyzing a Simulated Labor Market
code: # Wage and key variables summary_vars <- panel[, .(   mean_wage  = mean(lw),   sd_wage    = sd(lw),   mean_age   = mean(age),   mean_educ  = mean(educ),   mean_exper = mean(experience),   pct_female = mean(gender) )] t(summary_vars) #>                 [,1] #> mean_wage   7.930652 #> sd_wage     1.250121 #> mean_age   38.714200 #> mean_educ  16.113800 #> mean_exper 12.123640 #> pct_female  0.457000

title: Analyzing a Simulated Labor Market
code: hist(panel$lw, breaks = 50, col = "#3B82A0", border = "white",      main = "Distribution of Log Wages", xlab = "Log Wage")

title: Analyzing a Simulated Labor Market
code: panel[, .(mean_wage = mean(lw), n = .N), by = gender] #>    gender mean_wage     n #>     <num>     <num> <int> #> 1:      0  7.960131 13575 #> 2:      1  7.895626 11425

title: Analyzing a Simulated Labor Market
code: # A mover is someone who changes fid across periods movers <- panel[, .(n_firms = length(unique(fid))), by = i] movers[, mover := n_firms > 1] cat("Movers:", sum(movers$mover), "/", nrow(movers),     "(", round(100 * mean(movers$mover), 1), "%)\n") #> Movers: 2440 / 5000 ( 48.8 %)

title: Analyzing a Simulated Labor Market
text: estimate classic Abowd, Kramarz, Margolis (1999) two-way fixed effects decomposition: logwit=αi+ψj(,t)+εit\log w_{} = \alpha_i + \psi_{j(,t)} + \varepsilon_{}
code: library(fixest)  panel$fid_f <- as.factor(panel$fid) panel$i_f   <- as.factor(panel$i)  # Restrict to the largest connected set cf <- lfe::compfactor(list(panel$i_f, panel$fid_f)) largest <- names(which.max(table(cf))) panel_conn <- panel[cf == largest] panel_conn[, i_f   := droplevels(i_f)] panel_conn[, fid_f := droplevels(fid_f)]  cat("Observations in connected set:", nrow(panel_conn), "/", nrow(panel), "\n") #> Observations in connected set: 24970 / 25000  # Estimate model <- feols(lw ~ 1 | i_f + fid_f, data = panel_conn) #> NOTE: 0/1 fixed-effect singleton was removed (1 observation). summary(model) #> OLS estimation, Dep. Var.: lw #> Observations: 24,969 #> Fixed-effects: i_f: 4,994,  fid_f: 994 #> RMSE: 0.873302   Adj. R2: 0.35829

title: Analyzing a Simulated Labor Market
code: fe <- fixef(model) alpha_hat <- fe$i_f     # worker effects psi_hat   <- fe$fid_f   # firm effects  cat("Worker effects — mean:", round(mean(alpha_hat), 3),     " sd:", round(sd(alpha_hat), 3), "\n") #> Worker effects — mean: 8.25  sd: 0.814 cat("Firm effects   — mean:", round(mean(psi_hat), 3),     " sd:", round(sd(psi_hat), 3), "\n") #> Firm effects   — mean: -0.311  sd: 0.764

title: Analyzing a Simulated Labor Market
text: variance log wages decomposes : Var(logw)=Var(α̂)+Var(ψ̂)+2Cov(α̂,ψ̂)+Var(ε̂)\text{Var}(\log w) = \text{Var}(\hat\alpha) + \text{Var}(\hat\psi) + 2\,\text{Cov}(\hat\alpha, \hat\psi) + \text{Var}(\hat\varepsilon)
code: # Match FEs back to observations fe_list <- fixef(model) # Use fixef_id to map observations to fixed effects (handles singletons) alpha_vec <- fe_list$i_f[model$fixef_id$i_f] psi_vec   <- fe_list$fid_f[model$fixef_id$fid_f]  # Subset to model observations if singletons were removed obs_rm <- model$obs_selection$obsRemoved if (!is.null(obs_rm)) {   panel_conn <- panel_conn[obs_rm]  # negative indices = rows to drop }  panel_conn[, alpha_hat := alpha_vec] panel_conn[, psi_hat   := psi_vec] panel_conn[, resid     := lw - alpha_hat - psi_hat]  var_wage  <- var(panel_conn$lw) var_alpha <- var(panel_conn$alpha_hat) var_psi   <- var(panel_conn$psi_hat) cov_ap    <- cov(panel_conn$alpha_hat, panel_conn$psi_hat) var_resid <- var(panel_conn$resid)  decomp <- data.frame(   Component = c("Var(alpha)", "Var(psi)", "2*Cov(alpha,psi)", "Var(residual)", "Total"),   Value = round(c(var_alpha, var_psi, 2 * cov_ap, var_resid, var_wage), 4),   Share = round(c(var_alpha, var_psi, 2 * cov_ap, var_resid, var_wage) / var_wage * 100, 1) ) decomp #>          Component  Value Share #> 1       Var(alpha) 0.6628  42.4 #> 2         Var(psi) 0.5686  36.4 #> 3 2*Cov(alpha,psi) 0.0222   1.4 #> 4    Var(residual) 1.9142 122.4 #> 5            Total 1.5633 100.0

title: Analyzing a Simulated Labor Market
text: positive correlation indicates assortative matching — high-type workers sort high-paying firms.
code: cor_ap <- cor(panel_conn$alpha_hat, panel_conn$psi_hat) cat("Correlation(alpha, psi):", round(cor_ap, 3), "\n") #> Correlation(alpha, psi): 0.018

title: Analyzing a Simulated Labor Market
text: variance decomposition biased firms movers. use lmbias() correct :
code: panel_conn$xbeta <- 0  # intercept-only model  results <- lmbias(model, panel_conn, R = 50) print(round(results, 4)) #>           Var of i_f Var of fid_f Covariance Correlation #> Original      0.6628       0.5686     0.0111      0.0181 #> Bias          0.2896       0.1646    -0.1376     -0.6305 #> Corrected     0.3732       0.4040     0.1487      0.6485

title: Analyzing a Simulated Labor Market
text: bias correction typically reduces estimated variance worker firm effects (since limited mobility inflates ) can meaningfully change estimated sorting correlation.
code:
cat("--- Variance of Worker Effects ---
") #> --- Variance of Worker Effects --- cat("  Original: ", round(results["Original", 1], 4), "
") #>   Original:  0.6628 cat("  Bias:     ", round(results["Bias", 1], 4), "
") #>   Bias:      0.2896 cat("  Corrected:", round(results["Corrected", 1], 4), "

") #>   Corrected: 0.3732  cat("--- Variance of Firm Effects ---
") #> --- Variance of Firm Effects --- cat("  Original: ", round(results["Original", 2], 4), "
") #>   Original:  0.5686 cat("  Bias:     ", round(results["Bias", 2], 4), "
") #>   Bias:      0.1646 cat("  Corrected:", round(results["Corrected", 2], 4), "

") #>   Corrected: 0.404  cat("--- Correlation ---
") #> --- Correlation --- cat("  Original: ", round(results["Original", 4], 4), "
") #>   Original:  0.0181 cat("  Corrected:", round(results["Corrected", 4], 4), "
") #>   Corrected: 0.6485

title: Analyzing a Simulated Labor Market
text: workflow lets study parameters like mobility (lambda), number firm types (nk), sample size (ni) affect magnitude limited mobility bias — key concern empirical labor economics.

title: Introduction to labormarket
text: labormarket package provides tools : Simulating labor markets worker firm heterogeneity, sorting, gender bias Correcting limited mobility bias two-way fixed effects (AKM) wage decompositions

title: Introduction to labormarket
text: simlabormarket() function generates panel dataset workers firms. Key parameters:

title: Introduction to labormarket
text: returned LaborMarket object stores panel data generating parameters:
code: library(labormarket)  set.seed(123) lm_obj <- simlabormarket(nk = 4, nl = 5, nt = 3, ni = 500, lambda = 0.1) #> -- LaborMarket simulation --- #>  #>   Parameters #>     Firm types (nk):     4  #>     Worker types (nl):   5  #>     Time periods (nt):   3  #>     Individuals (ni):    500  #>     Female ratio:        0.45  #>     Mobility (lambda):   0.1  #>  #>   Summary statistics #>     Observations:        1500  #>     Mean wage:           7.076  #>     Mean firm effect:    2.133  #>     Mean worker effect:  4.938 panel <- lm_obj@panel head(panel) #> Key: <i, spell> #>        i     l     k     t   fid spell gender       psi    alpha     time_fe #>    <int> <num> <num> <int> <int> <num>  <num>     <num>    <num>       <num> #> 1:     1     1     1     1    28     0      0 0.6799806 5.397201 0.005807621 #> 2:     1     1     1     2    28     0      0 0.9973784 6.791738 0.032980083 #> 3:     1     1     1     3    28     0      0 1.2960007 6.055003 0.022608009 #> 4:     2     3     4     1    67     0      1 2.7277843 4.117644 0.005807621 #> 5:     2     3     4     2    67     0      1 2.9259664 5.357491 0.032980083 #> 6:     2     3     4     3    67     0      1 3.1947476 3.883151 0.022608009 #>          lw  educ   age experience #>       <num> <num> <num>      <num> #> 1: 5.251428     0     0          0 #> 2: 7.242395     0     0          0 #> 3: 7.762032     0     0          0 #> 4: 6.334311     0     0          0 #> 5: 8.297153     0     0          0 #> 6: 6.634714     0     0          0

title: Introduction to labormarket
text: mincer = TRUE, wages include returns education, experience, experience squared, addition worker firm effects: logwit=0.07⋅educi+0.02⋅expit−0.0005⋅expit2+αi+ψj(,t)+εit\log w_{} = 0.07 \cdot \text{educ}_i + 0.02 \cdot \text{exp}_{} - 0.0005 \cdot \text{exp}_{}^2 + \alpha_i + \psi_{j(,t)} + \varepsilon_{}
code: lm_mincer <- simlabormarket(nk = 4, nl = 5, nt = 3, ni = 500,                              lambda = 0.1, mincer = TRUE) #> -- LaborMarket simulation --- #>  #>   Parameters #>     Firm types (nk):     4  #>     Worker types (nl):   5  #>     Time periods (nt):   3  #>     Individuals (ni):    500  #>     Female ratio:        0.45  #>     Mobility (lambda):   0.1  #>  #>   Summary statistics #>     Observations:        1500  #>     Mean wage:           5.916  #>     Mean firm effect:    2.118  #>     Mean worker effect:  2.515  #>     Mean age:            37.9  #>     Mean education:      16.6  #>     Mean experience:     14 head(lm_mincer@panel[, .(i, t, age, educ, experience, lw)]) #> Key: <i> #>        i     t   age  educ experience       lw #>    <int> <int> <num> <num>      <num>    <num> #> 1:     1     1    34    15         13 6.819594 #> 2:     1     2    35    15          0 4.586625 #> 3:     1     3    36    15          1 5.852040 #> 4:     2     1    30     4         20 5.787266 #> 5:     2     2    31     4         21 4.086527 #> 6:     2     3    32     4         22 6.161698

title: Introduction to labormarket
text: classic Abowd, Kramarz, Margolis (1999) decomposition writes: logwit=αi+ψj(,t)+Xit′β+εit\log w_{} = \alpha_i + \psi_{j(,t)} + X_{}'\beta + \varepsilon_{} can estimate using fixest::feols():
code: library(fixest)  set.seed(42) lm_obj <- simlabormarket(nk = 2, nl = 2, nt = 5, ni = 2000, lambda = 0.5) #> -- LaborMarket simulation --- #>  #>   Parameters #>     Firm types (nk):     2  #>     Worker types (nl):   2  #>     Time periods (nt):   5  #>     Individuals (ni):    2000  #>     Female ratio:        0.45  #>     Mobility (lambda):   0.5  #>  #>   Summary statistics #>     Observations:        10000  #>     Mean wage:           7.122  #>     Mean firm effect:    2.308  #>     Mean worker effect:  4.817 panel <- lm_obj@panel panel$fid <- as.factor(panel$fid) panel$i   <- as.factor(panel$i)  # Restrict to the largest connected set cf <- lfe::compfactor(list(panel$i, panel$fid)) largest <- names(which.max(table(cf))) panel <- panel[cf == largest, ] panel$i   <- droplevels(panel$i) panel$fid <- droplevels(panel$fid)  model <- feols(lw ~ 1 | i + fid, data = panel) summary(model) #> OLS estimation, Dep. Var.: lw #> Observations: 10,000 #> Fixed-effects: i: 2,000,  fid: 500 #> RMSE: 1.10751   Adj. R2: 0.092137

title: Introduction to labormarket
text: number movers firms small, variance estimated fixed effects upward-biased. lmbias() function implements bootstrap correction Azkarate-Askasua Zerecero (2022). output 3x4 matrix: Original: Variance, covariance, correlation estimated fixed effects Bias: Estimated upward bias limited mobility Corrected: Bias-corrected estimates
code: # Add predicted values for the bootstrap panel$xbeta <- 0  # intercept-only model  # Run bias correction (small R for vignette speed) results <- lmbias(model, panel, R = 20) print(results) #>             Var of i Var of fid  Covariance Correlation #> Original  0.37190443  0.3775042  0.00865995  0.02311207 #> Bias      0.30312312  0.1141722 -0.05559410 -0.29884021 #> Corrected 0.06878132  0.2633321  0.06425405  0.32195228

title: Introduction to labormarket
text: Abowd, J. M., Kramarz, F., Margolis, D. N. (1999). “High Wage Workers High Wage Firms.” Econometrica, 67(2), 251-333. Azkarate-Askasua, M. Zerecero, M. (2022). “Macroeconomics Firms’ Financing.”

what: Tools for Learning How Labor Markets Work
title: Tools for Learning How Labor Markets Work
text: labormarket R package simulating worker-firm matched datasets correcting limited mobility bias two-way fixed effects models. designed teaching research tool labor economics.

title: Tools for Learning How Labor Markets Work
text: Install development version GitHub:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("hsantanna88/labormarket")

title: Tools for Learning How Labor Markets Work
code: library(labormarket)  # Simulate a labor market with 6 firm types, 10 worker types, 4 periods, 100k workers lm_obj <- simlabormarket(nk = 6, nl = 10, nt = 4, ni = 100000, lambda = 0.05)  # Access the panel data panel <- lm_obj@panel head(panel)

title: Tools for Learning How Labor Markets Work
text: fitting two-way fixed effects (AKM) model, use lmbias() correct limited mobility bias following Azkarate-Askasua Zerecero (2022):
code: library(fixest)  # Fit an AKM model panel$fid <- as.factor(panel$fid) panel$i   <- as.factor(panel$i) model <- feols(lw ~ 1 | i + fid, data = panel)  # Add predicted values panel$xbeta <- predict(model, type = "link")  # Bootstrap bias correction results <- lmbias(model, panel, R = 100) print(results)

title: Tools for Learning How Labor Markets Work
text: Azkarate-Askasua, M. Zerecero, M. (2022). “Macroeconomics Firms’ Financing.”

title: Tools for Learning How Labor Markets Work
text: MIT

what: S4 Class for Representing a Simulated Labor Market — LaborMarket-class
title: S4 Class for Representing a Simulated Labor Market — LaborMarket-class
text: class represent state simulated labor market including panel data, transition/steady-state matrices, generating parameters.

title: S4 Class for Representing a Simulated Labor Market — LaborMarket-class
text: panel data.table object representing simulated panel data. init.params list initial parameters (nk, nl, nt, ni, ratiog, lambda). tranM array representing transition matrices across firm types. steadyM array representing steady-state distributions. alpha_mean Numeric vector mean worker effects type. psi_mean Numeric vector mean firm effects type. psi_sd Standard deviation firm effect distribution. alpha_sd Numeric vector standard deviations worker effects type. csort Sorting effect parameter. cnetw Network effect parameter. csig Standard deviation moving probability. fsize Base firm size parameter. w_sigma Standard deviation wage shock. neduc Number education levels. sort_gap Sorting gap gender bias. shocks Numeric vector time-period shocks.

title: S4 Class for Representing a Simulated Labor Market — LaborMarket-class

what: Corrects the Limited Mobility Bias Using Bootstrap — lmbias
title: Corrects the Limited Mobility Bias Using Bootstrap — lmbias
text: lmbias function applies bootstrapping technique correct limited mobility bias (often referred small sample bias) fixed-effects models. implementation based method proposed Azkarate-Askasua Zerecero (2022). uses Rademacher random vector fixest package's model specifications calculations.

title: Corrects the Limited Mobility Bias Using Bootstrap — lmbias
code: lmbias(   femodel,   data,   vcov = "HC1",   R = 1000,   xvarname = "xbeta",   parallel = c("no", "multicore", "snow"),   cluster = NULL,   ncpus = getOption("boot.ncpus", 1L),   cl = NULL )

title: Corrects the Limited Mobility Bias Using Bootstrap — lmbias
text: femodel model object class fixest (fixest package) contains fixed effects estimates corrected. Must least two fixed effects. data data.frame data.table object contains dataset used fixed effects model. vcov character string specifying type heteroskedasticity correction used. Must one "HC0" "HC1" (default). R integer sets number bootstrap replications. Default 1000. xvarname character string giving name column data contains linear predictor (X * beta). Default "xbeta". parallel character string determines type parallel operation used (). Possible values "", "multicore", "snow". Default "". cluster optional vector factor provides data's cluster IDs cluster bootstrapping desired. ncpus integer specifying number CPUs used parallel processing. argument relevant parallel set "multicore" "snow". Default uses option set getOption("boot.ncpus", 1L). cl optional parallel snow cluster use parallel = "snow". supplied parallel = "snow", cluster local machine created.

title: Corrects the Limited Mobility Bias Using Bootstrap — lmbias
text: 3x4 matrix rows "Original", "Bias", "Corrected", columns variance fixed effect, covariance, correlation.

title: Corrects the Limited Mobility Bias Using Bootstrap — lmbias
text: Azkarate-Askasua, M. Zerecero, M. (2022). "Macroeconomics Firms' Financing." Working Paper. https://github.com/mazkarate/bias_correction

title: Corrects the Limited Mobility Bias Using Bootstrap — lmbias
code: # \donttest{ set.seed(1) # Simulate a labor market with high mobility for connectivity lm_sim <- simlabormarket(nk = 2, nl = 2, nt = 5, ni = 2000, lambda = 0.5) #> -- LaborMarket simulation --- #>  #>   Parameters #>     Firm types (nk):     2  #>     Worker types (nl):   2  #>     Time periods (nt):   5  #>     Individuals (ni):    2000  #>     Female ratio:        0.45  #>     Mobility (lambda):   0.5  #>  #>   Summary statistics #>     Observations:        10000  #>     Mean wage:           4.801  #>     Mean firm effect:    2.534  #>     Mean worker effect:  2.272  #>  panel <- lm_sim@panel panel$fid <- as.factor(panel$fid) panel$i   <- as.factor(panel$i)  # Restrict to the largest connected set cf <- lfe::compfactor(list(panel$i, panel$fid)) largest <- names(which.max(table(cf))) panel <- panel[cf == largest, ] panel$i   <- droplevels(panel$i) panel$fid <- droplevels(panel$fid)  # Fit a two-way fixed effects model model <- fixest::feols(lw ~ 1 | i + fid, data = panel)  # Add the linear predictor column (match model obs) panel <- panel[!is.na(predict(model, newdata = panel)), ] panel$xbeta <- 0  # Correct limited mobility bias (small R for demo) lmbias(model, panel, R = 50) #>             Var of i Var of fid   Covariance Correlation #> Original  0.18802624 0.26887455  0.002965913   0.0131909 #> Bias      0.13758109 0.05266605 -0.025543702  -0.3000814 #> Corrected 0.05044515 0.21620850  0.028509615   0.3132723 # }

what: Simulate a Labor Market with Gender Bias — simlabormarket
title: Simulate a Labor Market with Gender Bias — simlabormarket
text: function creates simulated labor market parameters control number firm types, fraction females labor market, percentage movers, etc. simulated panel includes worker firm fixed effects, match effects, optionally Mincer-style human capital variables (age, education, experience).

title: Simulate a Labor Market with Gender Bias — simlabormarket
code: simlabormarket(   nk = 6,   ratiog = 0.45,   lambda = 0.05,   nl = 10,   nt = 4,   ni = 1e+05,   mincer = FALSE )

title: Simulate a Labor Market with Gender Bias — simlabormarket
text: nk Integer >= 2. Number firm types clusters. ratiog Numeric (0, 1). Fraction females labor market. lambda Numeric (0, 1). Probability worker moving firms period. nl Integer >= 2. Number worker types clusters. nt Integer >= 2. Number time periods. ni Positive integer. Number individuals. mincer Logical. TRUE, wages generated using Mincer equation (returns education, experience, experience squared) together worker firm random effects. Default FALSE.

title: Simulate a Labor Market with Gender Bias — simlabormarket
text: LaborMarket S4 object containing simulated panel parameters.

title: Simulate a Labor Market with Gender Bias — simlabormarket
code: # Simulate a small labor market lm_obj <- simlabormarket(nk = 3, nl = 3, nt = 3, ni = 100, lambda = 0.3) #> -- LaborMarket simulation --- #>  #>   Parameters #>     Firm types (nk):     3  #>     Worker types (nl):   3  #>     Time periods (nt):   3  #>     Individuals (ni):    100  #>     Female ratio:        0.45  #>     Mobility (lambda):   0.3  #>  #>   Summary statistics #>     Observations:        300  #>     Mean wage:           4.48  #>     Mean firm effect:    1.684  #>     Mean worker effect:  2.794  #>   # With Mincer wage equation lm_mincer <- simlabormarket(nk = 3, nl = 3, nt = 3, ni = 100,                              lambda = 0.3, mincer = TRUE) #> -- LaborMarket simulation --- #>  #>   Parameters #>     Firm types (nk):     3  #>     Worker types (nl):   3  #>     Time periods (nt):   3  #>     Individuals (ni):    100  #>     Female ratio:        0.45  #>     Mobility (lambda):   0.3  #>  #>   Summary statistics #>     Observations:        300  #>     Mean wage:           5.916  #>     Mean firm effect:    1.733  #>     Mean worker effect:  3.006  #>     Mean age:            35.2  #>     Mean education:      16.1  #>     Mean experience:     9  #>
